title: Wet-felted pebble set
subtitle: A set of smooth, dense felt pebbles in natural wool colours
excerpt: Roll small quantities of merino roving into smooth pebble shapes by wet felting in the palms. The pebble form is the fastest and most satisfying introduction to three-dimensional wet felting: no resist, no cutting, just roving, soap, hot water, and rolling.
sourceNotes: The palm-rolling pebble form is a standard introductory exercise in wet-felting workshops. The technique is observation-led: a small amount of roving, soapy hot water, and circular rolling in the palms is the method consistently taught. No specific PD text source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique that uses hot water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibre scales to open, migrate, and permanently lock together into a non-woven fabric.

content.text: A set of five pebbles in natural undyed wool tones,

in the palms. Each pebble takes about 10 minutes to felt from raw roving to a firm, smooth, stable form. Use Corriedale or Shetland for a firm pebble with good density; Merino produces a softer pebble with a slightly fuzzier surface. Natural-coloured grey, cream, brown, and charcoal rovings produce the most convincing pebble palette.

items.name: Corriedale or Shetland roving (mixed natural colours)
items.qty: 10 g per pebble (50 g for a set of 5)

items.name: Tea towel for pressing out water

content.text: Pull off 10 g of roving. Fold it in half three times and roll it loosely between your palms into a rough ball, roughly 4-5 cm across. The ball will shrink to about 3-3.5 cm when fully felted.

Wet your palms with hot water. Rub the soap bar across the palm of one hand to leave a film of soap. Place the dry roving ball in the soapy palm. Squirt a little hot water over the ball and close your other hand over it. Press gently with both palms to wet the whole ball.

Begin rolling the ball in circular motions between flat palms. Use light pressure for the first 2 minutes: the fibres need time to settle into a round shape before you apply firm pressure. If you press hard too early, the ball flattens. After 2 minutes, increase the rolling pressure. Add more hot water and soap as the ball dries out during rolling. After 8-10 minutes, the ball should be firm, smooth, and noticeably smaller. Squeeze it: a well-felted pebble does not spring back much when compressed.

Rinse the finished pebble under cold running water. Squeeze out the water and press between the tea towel. Reshape by rolling briefly between dry palms to restore the round form. Dry on a rack or windowsill. Drying takes 2-4 hours for a pebble this size.

For a pebble with a contrasting core colour, wrap a small inner ball of one colour with an outer layer of roving in a contrasting colour before the first wetting. The inner colour will show slightly at any surface abrasion points over time, producing a natural weathered effect. For a striped pebble, lay thin wisps of contrasting-colour roving around the dry ball before the first wetting: press them in gently during the initial rolling phase before they have a chance to slide.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to palm-rolling felting.

items.symptom: Ball flattening into a disc rather than staying round
items.cause: Too much pressure applied too early before the fibres have initial cohesion.
items.fix: Reduce palm pressure for the first 2-3 minutes. The ball needs to reach a basic round form before firm rolling pressure helps rather than distorts.

items.symptom: Surface feels rough and scratchy rather than smooth
items.cause: The roving was a coarser breed (Romney or similar) rather than fine Merino or Corriedale, or the ball was not rolled long enough to bring the surface fibres to a smooth finish.
items.fix: Continue rolling for another 5 minutes with fresh hot water and soap. Firm, sustained circular rolling brings loose surface fibres into the body of the pebble.
